examples.explanation: Start with head=1. Current=1, no child, tail=1, move to next=2. Current=2, has child=6. Recursively flatten child list starting at 6. Inside recursion: current=6, no child, tail=6, move to next=7. Current=7, has child=9. Recursively flatten child list starting at 9. Inside: current=9, no child, tail=9, move to next=10. Current=10, no child, tail=10, move to next=null. Return tail=10 to 7's recursion. Connect 7.next=9, 9.prev=7, 10.next=8, 8.prev=10, set 7.child=null, tail=10. Current=8, no child, tail=8, move to next=null. Return tail=8 to 2's recursion. Connect 2.next=6, 6.prev=2, 8.next=3, 3.prev=8, set 2.child=null, tail=8. Back to main: current=3, no child, tail=3, move to next=4. Current=4, no child, tail=4, move to next=5. Current=5, no child, tail=5, move to next=null. Final list: 1->2->6->7->9->10->8->3->4->5.

Start head=1. Current=1, has child=2. Recursively flatten child list at 2. Inside: current=2, has child=4. Recursively flatten child list at 4. Inside: current=4, no child, tail=4, next=null, return tail=4. Back to 2: connect 2.next=4, 4.prev=2, 4.next=3, 3.prev=4, set 2.child=null, tail=4. Current=3, no child, tail=3, next=null, return tail=3 to 1. Connect 1.next=2, 2.prev=1, 3.next=null, set 1.child=null, tail=3. Final: 1->2->4->3.

approaches.name: Approach 1: Recursive Depth-First Traversal
approaches.summary: Traverse the list recursively, flattening child nodes before moving to the next node. This approach naturally handles nested structures by processing children first.
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(n) in worst case due to recursion stack

ladders.title: Full Problem — Flatten Multilevel Linked List Using Recursive DFS
ladders.desc: Complete implementation to flatten a multilevel linked list where each node has next, child, and value pointers.
ladders.explanation: The intuition behind this recursive DFS approach is to treat the multilevel list as a tree-like structure where each node's child pointer leads to a sublist. By recursively flattening child lists before processing the next sibling, we ensure all nested levels are properly integrated into a single-level list. This works because it follows depth-first traversal: when we encounter a node with a child, we fully process that child's entire chain before returning to continue with the original node's next pointer. The algorithm maintains a 'tail' pointer that tracks the last node in the currently flattened portion, allowing us to seamlessly attach flattened child lists. Step-by-step: 1. Define a recursive helper function flattenDFS(node) that returns the tail of the flattened list starting from node. 2. Initialize current = node, tail = node. 3. While current exists: a. Save current.next as nextNode. b. If current has a child: i. Recursively flatten the child list, getting childTail = flattenDFS(current.child). ii. Connect current.next = current.child, and current.child.prev = current (for doubly linked). iii. Connect childTail.next = nextNode, and if nextNode exists, nextNode.prev = childTail. iv. Set current.child = null. v. Update tail = childTail (or current if childTail is null). c. Else, tail = current. d. Move current = nextNode. 4. Return tail. Time complexity is O(n) where n is total nodes, as each node is visited once. Space complexity is O(n) worst-case due to recursion stack depth equal to maximum nesting level. Connection to L1: This full solution calls the recursive helper function repeatedly. Common mistakes include not properly handling the tail pointer updates, forgetting to nullify child pointers after flattening, or infinite recursion when cycles exist (though problem assumes no cycles). Real code pattern: def flatten(head): def dfs(node): curr, tail = node, node while curr: nxt = curr.next if curr.child: child_tail = dfs(curr.child) curr.next = curr.child curr.child.prev = curr child_tail.next = nxt if nxt: nxt.prev = child_tail curr.child = None tail = child_tail else: tail = curr curr = nxt return tail if head: dfs(head) return head.

examples.trace: Start with head=1. Current=1, no child, tail=1, move to next=2. Current=2, has child=6. Recursively flatten child list starting at 6. Inside recursion: current=6, no child, tail=6, move to next=7. Current=7, has child=9. Recursively flatten child list starting at 9. Inside: current=9, no child, tail=9, move to next=10. Current=10, no child, tail=10, move to next=null. Return tail=10 to 7's recursion. Connect 7.next=9, 9.prev=7, 10.next=8, 8.prev=10, set 7.child=null, tail=10. Current=8, no child, tail=8, move to next=null. Return tail=8 to 2's recursion. Connect 2.next=6, 6.prev=2, 8.next=3, 3.prev=8, set 2.child=null, tail=8. Back to main: current=3, no child, tail=3, move to next=4. Current=4, no child, tail=4, move to next=5. Current=5, no child, tail=5, move to next=null. Final list: 1->2->6->7->9->10->8->3->4->5.

Start head=1. Current=1, has child=2. Recursively flatten child list at 2. Inside: current=2, has child=4. Recursively flatten child list at 4. Inside: current=4, no child, tail=4, next=null, return tail=4. Back to 2: connect 2.next=4, 4.prev=2, 4.next=3, 3.prev=4, set 2.child=null, tail=4. Current=3, no child, tail=3, next=null, return tail=3 to 1. Connect 1.next=2, 2.prev=1, 3.next=null, set 1.child=null, tail=3. Final: 1->2->4->3.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Recursive DFS Helper Function
ladders.desc: Implement a function that recursively flattens a multilevel list starting from a given node and returns the tail.
ladders.explanation: The intuition for this helper function is that it processes a segment of the multilevel list in isolation, flattening all nested child lists within that segment and returning the last node. This modular approach allows the main function to connect these flattened segments seamlessly. It works by traversing the list linearly while recursively diving into child lists whenever encountered, ensuring depth-first order. Step-by-step: 1. Function dfs(node): if node is null, return null. 2. Initialize current = node, tail = node. 3. While current is not null: a. Save current.next as nextNode. b. If current.child exists: i. Recursively call dfs(current.child) to get childTail. ii. Connect current.next = current.child, and current.child.prev = current. iii. Connect childTail.next = nextNode, and if nextNode exists, nextNode.prev = childTail. iv. Set current.child = null. v. Update tail = childTail (or current if childTail is null). c. Else, update tail = current. d. Set current = nextNode. 4. Return tail. Time complexity is O(m) where m is nodes in this segment, as each node is processed once. Space complexity is O(d) for recursion depth, where d is maximum nesting in this segment. Connection to L0: This helper is called repeatedly in the full solution to handle each child list. Common mistakes include not updating tail correctly after flattening a child, or infinite recursion if child points to ancestor (but problem assumes no cycles). Real code pattern: def dfs(node): curr, tail = node, node while curr: nxt = curr.next if curr.child: child_tail = dfs(curr.child) curr.next = curr.child curr.child.prev = curr child_tail.next = nxt if nxt: nxt.prev = child_tail curr.child = None tail = child_tail else: tail = curr curr = nxt return tail.

examples.input: Node: 2 -> 3 -> null with child 6 -> 7 -> 8 -> null (7 has child 9 -> 10 -> null)
examples.trace: Start dfs(2). Current=2, has child=6. Recursively dfs(6). Inside: current=6, no child, tail=6, next=7. Current=7, has child=9. Recursively dfs(9). Inside: current=9, no child, tail=9, next=10. Current=10, no child, tail=10, next=null. Return tail=10 to 7. Connect 7.next=9, 9.prev=7, 10.next=8, 8.prev=10, set 7.child=null, tail=10. Current=8, no child, tail=8, next=null. Return tail=8 to 2. Connect 2.next=6, 6.prev=2, 8.next=3, 3.prev=8, set 2.child=null, tail=8. Current=3, no child, tail=3, next=null. Return tail=3.
Start dfs(5). Current=5, no child, tail=5, next=null. Return tail=5.

testCases.input: 1 -> 2 -> null with child 3 -> null at node 1
1 -> 2 -> null with child 3 -> 4 -> null at node 2
1 -> 2 -> 3 -> null with child 4 -> null at node 2
1 -> null with child 2 -> null with child 3 -> null at node 2
1 -> 2 -> null with child 3 -> null at node 1 and child 4 -> null at node 2
1 -> 2 -> 3 -> 4 -> null with child 5 -> 6 -> null at node 3

ladders.title: Core Logic — Depth-First Traversal with Tail Tracking
ladders.desc: Implement linear traversal of a linked list while recursively processing child lists and maintaining a tail pointer.
ladders.explanation: The intuition behind this core logic is that flattening a multilevel list requires visiting nodes in depth-first order: when we see a child, we must fully process its entire chain before continuing with the original next pointer. The tail pointer is crucial because it tracks the end of the currently flattened portion, allowing us to attach the flattened child list and then resume with the saved next node. This works by iterating through nodes linearly, but whenever a child is found, we pause the current iteration, recursively flatten the child list (which updates the tail), then connect the child list between the current node and the saved next node. Step-by-step: 1. Initialize current = head, tail = head. 2. While current is not null: a. Save current.next as nextNode. b. If current.child is not null: i. Recursively flatten current.child (this returns childTail). ii. Set current.next = current.child, and current.child.prev = current. iii. Set childTail.next = nextNode, and if nextNode exists, nextNode.prev = childTail. iv. Set current.child = null. v. Update tail = childTail. c. Else, update tail = current. d. Set current = nextNode. 3. The list is now flattened. Time complexity is O(n) as each node is visited once. Space complexity is O(d) for recursion depth. Connection to L1: This logic is encapsulated in the helper function. Common mistakes include losing reference to nextNode before recursion, not updating tail after flattening, or incorrectly handling prev pointers in doubly linked lists. Real code pattern: curr, tail = head, head while curr: nxt = curr.next if curr.child: child_tail = flatten(curr.child) curr.next = curr.child curr.child.prev = curr child_tail.next = nxt if nxt: nxt.prev = child_tail curr.child = None tail = child_tail else: tail = curr curr = nxt.

examples.input: List: 1 -> 2 -> 3 -> null, with child 4 -> 5 -> null at node 2
examples.trace: Start: current=1, tail=1, no child, tail=1, move to next=2. Current=2, save next=3, has child=4. Recursively flatten child: current=4, tail=4, no child, tail=4, next=5. Current=5, tail=5, no child, tail=5, next=null. Return tail=5. Connect 2.next=4, 4.prev=2, 5.next=3, 3.prev=5, set 2.child=null, tail=5. Current=3, tail=3, no child, tail=3, next=null. Final tail=3.

examples.input: List: 1 -> null, with child 2 -> 3 -> null at node 1
examples.trace: Start: current=1, tail=1, save next=null, has child=2. Recursively flatten child: current=2, tail=2, no child, tail=2, next=3. Current=3, tail=3, no child, tail=3, next=null. Return tail=3. Connect 1.next=2, 2.prev=1, 3.next=null, set 1.child=null, tail=3. Current=null, done.

testCases.input: 1 -> 2 -> null, child 3 -> null at node 1 and child 4 -> null at node 2
1 -> null, child 2 -> null, child 3 -> null at node 2

ladders.title: Building Block — Recursive List Traversal with Side Effects
ladders.desc: Traverse a linked list recursively, modifying next pointers to integrate another list.
ladders.explanation: The intuition for this building block is that recursion allows us to handle nested structures naturally by diving deep into child lists before continuing with siblings. When we recursively process a node, we aim to flatten its child list and attach it in place, which requires modifying pointers as side effects. This works because each recursive call handles a sublist independently, returning its tail so the parent call can connect it properly. The key is that recursion follows the call stack: when we encounter a child, we pause the current level, fully process the child list, then return to connect it. Step-by-step: 1. Define function process(node): if node is null, return null. 2. Save node.next as nextNode. 3. If node.child exists: a. Call process(node.child) recursively, which returns childTail. b. Set node.next = node.child, and node.child.prev = node. c. Set childTail.next = nextNode, and if nextNode exists, nextNode.prev = childTail. d. Set node.child = null. e. Return process(nextNode) or handle tail. 4. Else, return process(nextNode) or node. Time complexity is O(n) for the list segment. Space complexity is O(d) for recursion depth. Connection to L2: This is the recursive mechanism used in the core logic. Common mistakes include not saving nextNode before recursion (leading to lost nodes), or not handling base cases correctly. Real code pattern: def process(node): if not node: return None nxt = node.next if node.child: child_tail = process(node.child) node.next = node.child node.child.prev = node child_tail.next = nxt if nxt: nxt.prev = child_tail node.child = None return process(nxt) if nxt else child_tail else: return process(nxt) if nxt else node.

examples.input: Node: 1 with child 2 -> 3 -> null, next 4 -> null
examples.trace: Call process(1). Save next=4. Has child=2. Recursively process(2). Save next=3. No child. Recursively process(3). Save next=null. No child. Return 3 to process(2). Set 2.next=null? Wait, correct: In process(1): after process(2) returns, connect 1.next=2, 2.prev=1, childTail=3 from process(2), set 3.next=4, 4.prev=3, set 1.child=null. Then call process(4) which returns 4. Final tail=4.

examples.input: Node: 5 with no child, next null
examples.trace: Call process(5). Save next=null. No child. Return 5.

testCases.input: 1 -> null, child 2 -> null, child 3 -> null at node 2
1 -> 2 -> null, child 3 -> null at node 1, child 4 -> null at node 2

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Linking Two Linked Lists
ladders.desc: Given two linked lists, insert the second list after a node in the first list.
ladders.explanation: The intuition for this basic operation is that to flatten a multilevel list, we need to insert child lists into the main list at specific points. This requires connecting the head of the child list after the current node, and connecting the tail of the child list to the original next node. This works by manipulating next (and prev for doubly linked) pointers: we set current.next to childHead, then find childTail and set childTail.next to nextNode. This seamlessly integrates the child list into the main flow. Step-by-step: 1. Given current node (with nextNode as current.next), childHead (start of child list), and childTail (end of child list). 2. Set current.next = childHead. If doubly linked, set childHead.prev = current. 3. Set childTail.next = nextNode. If doubly linked and nextNode exists, set nextNode.prev = childTail. 4. Optionally, set current.child = null if needed. Time complexity is O(1) for the linking itself, but O(m) to find childTail if not given (where m is child list length). Space complexity is O(1). Connection to L3: This operation is performed each time a child list is flattened in the recursive traversal. Common mistakes include not saving nextNode before linking (losing reference), or not updating prev pointers in doubly linked lists. Real code pattern: def insertList(current, childHead, childTail): nextNode = current.next current.next = childHead if childHead: childHead.prev = current childTail.next = nextNode if nextNode: nextNode.prev = childTail current.child = None # if applicable.

examples.input: Current node: 1 with next=4, child list: 2 -> 3 -> null (head=2, tail=3)
examples.trace: Save nextNode=4. Set 1.next=2. Set 2.prev=1. Set 3.next=4. Set 4.prev=3. Set 1.child=null. Result: 1->2->3->4.

examples.input: Current node: 5 with next=null, child list: 6 -> null (head=6, tail=6)
examples.trace: Save nextNode=null. Set 5.next=6. Set 6.prev=5. Set 6.next=null. Set 5.child=null. Result: 5->6.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Singly Linked List Traversal
ladders.desc: Traverse a singly linked list from head to tail, accessing each node's value and next pointer.
ladders.explanation: The intuition for this foundation concept is that linked lists are linear data structures where each node points to the next, allowing sequential access. Traversal is fundamental because to process or modify a list, we need to visit each node in order. This works by starting at the head node and repeatedly following the next pointer until reaching null, which signifies the end of the list. Step-by-step: 1. Initialize a variable current = head. 2. While current is not null: a. Access current.value or perform operation on current. b. Set current = current.next to move to the next node. 3. When current becomes null, traversal is complete. Time complexity is O(n) where n is number of nodes, as each node is visited once. Space complexity is O(1) as only a constant amount of extra space is used. Connection to L4: Traversal is used to find the tail of a list or to iterate through nodes when linking lists. Common mistakes include infinite loops if next pointers form a cycle (not in this problem), or off-by-one errors when stopping conditions are incorrect. Real code pattern: def traverse(head): current = head while current: # Do something with current.value current = current.next.

examples.trace: Start current=1. Visit node 1. Set current=2. Visit node 2. Set current=3. Visit node 3. Set current=null. Stop.
Start current=5. Visit node 5. Set current=null. Stop.

approaches.name: Approach 2: Iterative Stack-Based Traversal
approaches.summary: Use an explicit stack to traverse nodes, pushing next nodes when encountering child nodes to process later. This avoids recursion overhead and handles deep nesting iteratively.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Flatten Multilevel Linked List Using Stack
ladders.desc: Complete iterative stack-based solution to flatten a multi-level linked list.
ladders.explanation: The intuition behind this approach is to traverse the multi-level linked list depth-first while maintaining explicit control over the traversal order using a stack. Unlike recursion, which uses the call stack implicitly, we push nodes onto an explicit stack when we encounter child nodes, allowing us to process them later while continuing with the current path. This is particularly useful for deep nesting where recursion might cause stack overflow. The algorithm starts by initializing a stack with the head node. We then iterate while the stack is not empty: pop the top node, if it has a child, push its next node (if exists) onto the stack, then set the child as the next node and push the child onto the stack. This ensures that child nodes are processed before their original next siblings, mimicking depth-first traversal. The time complexity is O(n) because each node is pushed and popped exactly once from the stack. The space complexity is O(n) in the worst case when the list is deeply nested, as the stack might hold all nodes. This approach avoids recursion overhead and provides a clear, iterative control flow. Common mistakes include not handling null pointers properly when setting next pointers or forgetting to push the original next node before processing the child. The connection to L1 is that this full solution integrates the key subroutine of stack management to achieve the flattening. Real code pattern: Initialize stack with head, while stack not empty: pop current, if current has child: if current.next exists: push current.next, set current.next = current.child, push current.child, set current.child = null.

examples.trace:
Step 1: Initialize stack = [1]. Pop 1, child = 6, next = 2. Push 2, set 1.next = 6, push 6, set 1.child = null. Stack = [2,6].
Step 2: Pop 6, child = 9, next = 7. Push 7, set 6.next = 9, push 9, set 6.child = null. Stack = [2,7,9].
Step 3: Pop 9, child = null, next = 10. No push, set 9.next = 10. Stack = [2,7].
Step 4: Pop 7, child = null, next = 8. No push, set 7.next = 8. Stack = [2].
Step 5: Pop 2, child = null, next = 3. No push, set 2.next = 3. Stack = [].
Step 6: Stack empty, flattening complete. Result: 1->6->9->10->7->8->2->3->4->5->null.

examples.input:
A -> B -> C -> null
|
D -> E -> null
     |
     F -> null
examples.output: A -> D -> E -> F -> B -> C -> null
examples.trace:
Step 1: Stack = [A]. Pop A, child = D, next = B. Push B, set A.next = D, push D, set A.child = null. Stack = [B,D].
Step 2: Pop D, child = null, next = E. No push, set D.next = E. Stack = [B].
Step 3: Pop B, child = null, next = C. No push, set B.next = C. Stack = [].
Step 4: Stack empty, done. Result: A->D->E->B->C->null.

testCases.input:
A -> B -> C -> null
|
D -> E -> null
|    |
F    G -> null
testCases.expected: A -> D -> F -> E -> G -> B -> C -> null

ladders.title: Key Sub-Routine — Stack-Based Node Processing
ladders.desc: Process a node in the multi-level list using a stack to manage traversal order.
ladders.explanation: The intuition for this subroutine is to handle a single node by examining its child and next pointers, using a stack to defer processing of the next node when a child is present. This ensures depth-first traversal without recursion. The algorithm for processing a node 'curr' is: if curr has a child, push curr.next onto the stack (if it exists), then set curr.next to curr.child, push curr.child onto the stack, and set curr.child to null. If curr has no child, simply move to curr.next. The stack stores nodes that need to be processed later, specifically the original next nodes that are bypassed when following child pointers. This subroutine is the core of the iterative flattening process, as it defines how each node is expanded and how the stack maintains the traversal path. Time complexity per node is O(1) for push/pop operations, and space complexity depends on the stack size, which can be O(n) in worst-case nesting. Common mistakes include not checking for null before pushing curr.next or forgetting to nullify the child pointer after moving it. The connection to L0 is that this subroutine is repeatedly applied in a loop until the stack is empty to flatten the entire list. Real code pattern: def process_node(curr, stack): if curr.child: if curr.next: stack.append(curr.next), curr.next = curr.child, stack.append(curr.child), curr.child = None.

examples.trace:
Step 1: curr = 1, child = 2, next = 3. Since child exists, push next 3 onto stack: stack = [3].
Step 2: Set 1.next = 2.
Step 3: Push child 2 onto stack: stack = [3,2].
Step 4: Set 1.child = null. Done.

Step 1: curr = 5, child = null, next = 6. Since no child, no push to stack.
Step 2: Simply set curr = curr.next (5.next=6). Stack remains [7]. Done.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Depth-First Traversal with Stack
ladders.desc: Use a stack to perform depth-first traversal of a nested structure iteratively.
ladders.explanation: The intuition behind this core logic is that a stack's Last-In-First-Out (LIFO) property naturally supports depth-first traversal by allowing us to explore child nodes immediately while postponing sibling nodes. In the context of a multi-level linked list, when we encounter a node with a child, we want to process the child before the node's original next sibling. By pushing the next node onto the stack and then pushing the child, we ensure that the child is processed next (since it's on top of the stack), and the original next will be processed after the child's subtree is complete. This mimics recursive depth-first search without function calls. The algorithm involves initializing a stack with the starting node, then in a loop: pop a node, process it (handle child and next as in L1), and continue until the stack is empty. This logic is fundamental to flattening because it defines the order in which nodes are visited: always go deep into children first. Time complexity is O(n) as each node is processed once, and space is O(n) for the stack. Common mistakes include incorrect push order (e.g., pushing child before next) which would alter traversal order. The connection to L1 is that this logic repeatedly applies the node processing subroutine. Real code pattern: stack = [head], while stack: curr = stack.pop(), process curr with stack.

examples.trace:
Step 1: Stack = [1]. Pop 1, child = 3, next = 2. Push 2, set 1.next = 3, push 3. Stack = [2,3].
Step 2: Pop 3, no child, next = null. Stack = [2].
Step 3: Pop 2, no child, next = null. Stack = []. Done.

examples.input: List: A -> B -> null, child at A: C -> D -> null
examples.trace:
Step 1: Stack = [A]. Pop A, child = C, next = B. Push B, set A.next = C, push C. Stack = [B,C].
Step 2: Pop C, no child, next = D. Stack = [B].
Step 3: Pop D, no child, next = null. Stack = [B].
Step 4: Pop B, no child, next = null. Stack = []. Done.

testCases.input: A -> B -> null, child at B: C -> null
X -> Y -> null, child at X: Z -> null, child at Z: W -> null
testCases.expected: Traversal order: X, Z, W, Y

testCases.input: A -> B -> C -> null, child at A: D -> null, child at B: E -> null
testCases.expected: Traversal order: A, D, B, E, C

testCases.input: 1 -> 2 -> null, child at 1: 3 -> 4 -> null, child at 3: 5 -> null
P -> Q -> R -> null, child at P: S -> null, child at Q: T -> U -> null
testCases.expected: Traversal order: P, S, Q, T, U, R

ladders.title: Building Block — Stack Operations for Deferred Processing
ladders.desc: Use stack push and pop to defer processing of nodes in a specific order.
ladders.explanation: The intuition for this building block is that a stack allows us to save nodes for later processing, which is essential when we need to interrupt the current traversal path to explore a different branch. In the flattening problem, when we move from a node to its child, we defer the node's original next sibling by pushing it onto the stack. Later, when the child's subtree is fully processed, we pop from the stack to resume with the sibling. This deferred processing ensures that we traverse depth-first: children before siblings. The key operations are push (to save a node) and pop (to retrieve the most recently saved node). By pushing the next node before the child, we guarantee that after processing the child's entire chain, we return to the next node. This building block is a fundamental pattern in iterative DFS algorithms. Time complexity for push/pop is O(1), and space is proportional to the number of deferred nodes. Common mistakes include pushing nodes in the wrong order or not checking for null before pushing. The connection to L2 is that this block is used within the traversal loop to manage the order of node visits. Real code pattern: stack.append(node) to defer, node = stack.pop() to process deferred.

examples.trace:
Step 1: To defer next 2, push 2: stack = [2].
Step 2: To process child 3 next, push 3: stack = [2,3].
Now 3 is on top, so it will be processed before 2.

examples.input: Stack: [4,5], need to process next node
examples.trace:
Step 1: Pop from stack: top is 5, so node = 5.
Step 2: Stack becomes [4]. Now process node 5.

testCases.input: Push node A to empty stack
Push P, then Q to stack [R]

testCases.expected: First pop: U, Stack: [S,T]; Second pop: T, Stack: [S]

testCases.input: Push A, pop, push B to stack []
testCases.expected: After push A: [A]; after pop: node=A, stack=[]; after push B: [B]

testCases.input: Push X, Y, Z to empty stack, then pop one
testCases.expected: After pushes: [X,Y,Z]; after pop: node=Z, stack=[X,Y]

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Linked List Node Manipulation
ladders.desc: Perform basic operations on a linked list node: access and modify next and child pointers.
ladders.explanation: The intuition for this basic operation is that a node in a multi-level linked list has two pointers: 'next' (to the next node in the same level) and 'child' (to the head of a child list). Manipulating these pointers is essential for flattening, as we need to rewire them to transform the nested structure into a single-level list. Key operations include: accessing curr.next and curr.child to check their values, setting curr.next to a new node (e.g., to point to the child), and setting curr.child to null after moving it. This requires careful pointer management to avoid losing references or creating cycles. For example, when we set curr.next = curr.child, we must save the original curr.next first if we need it later (which we do by pushing it onto the stack). This operation is fundamental because it directly alters the list structure. Time complexity is O(1) per operation, and space is O(1). Common mistakes include dereferencing null pointers or not updating pointers in the correct order. The connection to L3 is that these manipulations are performed on nodes that are pushed/popped from the stack. Real code pattern: if curr.child: temp = curr.next, curr.next = curr.child, curr.child = None.

examples.trace:
Step 1: Save original next: temp = 2.
Step 2: Set 1.next = 3.
Step 3: Set 1.child = null.
Now node 1 points to 3 instead of 2, and child is cleared.

Step 1: Save original next: temp = null.
Step 2: Set A.next = B.
Step 3: Set A.child = null.
Now A points to B, and child is cleared.

testCases.expected: Manipulate: save next=B, set A.next=C, set A.child=null
Manipulate: save next=null, set M.next=N, set M.child=null

testCases.input: Node: X (next=Y, child=Z), clear child only
Node: P (next=Q, child=R), set next to child without save
Node: A (next=null, child=B), set next to child
Node: 9 (next=10, child=11), save next, set next to child, clear child

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Linked List Node Structure
ladders.desc: Understand the basic structure of a node in a multi-level linked list.
ladders.explanation: The intuition for this foundation concept is that a multi-level linked list is built from nodes, where each node contains data and pointers to other nodes. Specifically, each node has three components: a value (e.g., an integer or string), a 'next' pointer that references the next node in the same horizontal level, and a 'child' pointer that references the head of a nested sub-list. This structure allows for hierarchical organization, similar to a tree but with only one child per node (forming a linked list of linked lists). Understanding this node structure is crucial because flattening involves traversing and modifying these pointers. For example, a node with value 1, next pointing to node 2, and child pointing to node 3, represents a list where 1 is connected to 2 horizontally, but also has a vertical connection to 3. This foundation is the simplest prerequisite, as it defines the data we work with. There is no algorithm here; it's about recognizing the node fields. Time and space are not applicable at this conceptual level. Common mistakes include confusing next and child pointers or assuming multiple children. The connection to L4 is that once we understand the node structure, we can perform operations on its pointers. Real code pattern: class Node: def __init__(self, val): self.val = val, self.next = None, self.child = None.

examples.input: Node with value 5, no next or child
examples.trace: This node exists in isolation. It has a value of 5, but its next and child pointers are null, meaning it doesn't point to any other nodes.

examples.input: Node with value A, next points to node B, child points to node C
examples.trace: Node A contains the value 'A'. Its next pointer references node B, so A is connected to B in the same level. Its child pointer references node C, so A has a nested list starting with C.

testCases.input: Node value: P, next: Q, child: R
Node value: 100, next: null, child: null
Node value: A, next: B, child: null
Node value: M, next: null, child: N

approaches.name: Approach 3: In-Place Flattening with Pointer Manipulation
approaches.summary: Traverse the list and directly rewire pointers to flatten it in place without extra data structures. This method modifies the original list structure efficiently.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Flatten Multilevel Linked List In-Place
ladders.desc: Complete solution to flatten a multilevel linked list by directly rewiring pointers without extra data structures.
ladders.explanation: This problem requires flattening a multilevel linked list where each node has next, child, and prev pointers (doubly linked). The goal is to transform the hierarchical structure into a single-level doubly linked list in-place. The intuition is to traverse the list depth-first: whenever we encounter a node with a child, we need to insert the entire child list between the current node and its next node. This requires careful pointer manipulation to maintain the doubly linked structure. The algorithm starts at the head and iterates through nodes. For each node: if it has a child, we find the tail of the child list, then rewire pointers: set current.next.prev to child's tail (if current.next exists), set child.prev to current, set current.next to child, set current.child to null, and set child's tail.next to the original next. This effectively splices the child list into the main list. We then continue from the newly inserted nodes. The time complexity is O(n) where n is the total number of nodes, as each node is visited once. Space complexity is O(1) since we only use a few pointers. This approach is efficient because it modifies the list in place without recursion or stacks. Connection to L1: This full solution relies on the key subroutine of finding the tail of a child list. Common mistakes include not updating prev pointers correctly in doubly linked lists, forgetting to nullify child pointers after flattening, or mishandling edge cases like empty lists or nodes without children. Real code pattern: def flatten(head): current = head; while current: if current.child: child_tail = find_tail(current.child); if current.next: current.next.prev = child_tail; child_tail.next = current.next; current.next = current.child; current.child.prev = current; current.child = null; current = current.next; return head.

examples.trace: Start: 1→2→3→4→5→6, child at 2: 7→8→9→10, child at 8: 11→12. Step 1: At node 1, no child, move to 2. Step 2: At node 2, has child 7. Find tail of child list: start at 7→8→9→10, tail is 10. Rewire: 2.next becomes 7, 7.prev becomes 2, 10.next becomes 3, 3.prev becomes 10, 2.child becomes null. List: 1→2→7→8→9→10→3→4→5→6. Step 3: Move to 7, no child, move to 8. Step 4: At node 8, has child 11. Find tail: 11→12, tail is 12. Rewire: 8.next becomes 11, 11.prev becomes 8, 12.next becomes 9, 9.prev becomes 12, 8.child becomes null. List: 1→2→7→8→11→12→9→10→3→4→5→6. Step 5: Continue through remaining nodes (9,10,3,4,5,6), no children. Final flattened list.

Start: 1→2, child at 1: 3→4. Step 1: At node 1, has child 3. Find tail: 3→4, tail is 4. Rewire: 1.next becomes 3, 3.prev becomes 1, 4.next becomes 2, 2.prev becomes 4, 1.child becomes null. List: 1→3→4→2. Step 2: Move through 3,4,2, no children. Final flattened list.

ladders.title: Key Sub-Routine — Find Tail of a Doubly Linked List
ladders.desc: Helper function to find the last node in a doubly linked list.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is crucial for the in-place flattening algorithm because when we insert a child list into the main list, we need to know where the child list ends to properly connect it to the next node in the main list. The intuition is simple: start from the head of the list and traverse using the next pointer until we reach a node where next is null—that node is the tail. For a doubly linked list, we also ensure the tail's prev pointer is correctly set (though in this context, we're mainly concerned with the next chain). The algorithm: initialize current = head; while current.next is not null: current = current.next; return current. Time complexity is O(k) where k is the length of the list, and space is O(1). Connection to L0: This function is called whenever a node has a child to find the tail of the child list before rewiring. Common mistakes include not handling empty lists (head is null) or infinite loops if the list is circular (but problem assumes acyclic). Real code pattern: def find_tail(node): if not node: return null; while node.next: node = node.next; return node.

examples.trace: Start at node 1. Step 1: Check 1.next = 2, not null, move to 2. Step 2: Check 2.next = 3, not null, move to 3. Step 3: Check 3.next = null, so tail is 3.
Start at node 5. Step 1: Check 5.next = null, so tail is 5.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Insert Child List Between Nodes in Doubly Linked List
ladders.desc: The central technique of rewiring pointers to splice a child list into the main list.
ladders.explanation: This is the heart of the in-place flattening approach. The intuition is to treat the child list as a segment that needs to be inserted between the current node and its next node in the main list. For a doubly linked list, we must update four pointers: current.next, child.head.prev, child.tail.next, and original next.prev (if next exists). The algorithm: given current node with child, find child_tail using find_tail. Store original_next = current.next. Then: set current.next = child; set child.prev = current; set child_tail.next = original_next; if original_next is not null: set original_next.prev = child_tail; set current.child = null. This ensures the doubly linked properties are maintained. Time complexity is O(1) for the pointer updates plus O(k) for finding the tail, but overall O(n) in full context. Space is O(1). Connection to L1: This logic uses the tail found by the helper function. Common mistakes include forgetting to update prev pointers (breaking doubly linked structure), not handling cases where current.next is null, or not nullifying child pointer after insertion. Real code pattern: if current.child: child_tail = find_tail(current.child); next_node = current.next; current.next = current.child; current.child.prev = current; child_tail.next = next_node; if next_node: next_node.prev = child_tail; current.child = null.

examples.input: Main: A - B, insert child C - D between A and B
examples.trace: Start: A→B, child at A: C→D. Step 1: Find child_tail: start at C, C.next = D, D.next = null, so tail is D. Step 2: Store original_next = B. Step 3: Rewire: A.next = C, C.prev = A, D.next = B, B.prev = D, A.child = null. Result: A→C→D→B, with prev pointers: A←C←D←B.

examples.input: Main: X - null, insert child Y - Z after X
examples.trace: Start: X→null, child at X: Y→Z. Step 1: Find child_tail: Y→Z, tail is Z. Step 2: Store original_next = null. Step 3: Rewire: X.next = Y, Y.prev = X, Z.next = null, no update to original_next.prev, X.child = null. Result: X→Y→Z.

testCases.input: Main: a-b-c, Child: d-e, insert at a
Main: p-q, Child: r, insert at p
Main: x-y-z, Child: null, insert at x
Main: m-n, Child: o-p, insert at m
Main: a-b, Child: c, insert at b
Main: null, Child: 1-2, insert at null

ladders.title: Building Block — Traverse Doubly Linked List with Child Pointers
ladders.desc: Iterating through a multilevel linked list while handling child nodes.
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept involves navigating a doubly linked list where nodes may have child pointers that lead to sublists. The intuition is to perform a depth-first traversal: we move along the next pointers, but when we encounter a child, we need to explore that child list before continuing with the original next. However, for in-place flattening, we don't recursively descend; instead, we insert the child list immediately. The algorithm: start at head, use a while loop: while current is not null: if current has child: perform insertion logic (as in L2), then move to current.next (which now points to the child head). Else: move to current.next. This ensures we process all nodes in flattened order. Time complexity is O(n) as each node is visited once. Space is O(1). Connection to L2: This traversal uses the insertion logic when a child is found. Common mistakes include getting stuck in infinite loops if not moving pointer correctly after insertion, or missing nodes if child list is not fully traversed. Real code pattern: current = head; while current: if current.child: # insertion logic; current = current.next.

examples.trace: Start at 1. Step 1: current = 1, no child, move to next: 2. Step 2: current = 2, has child 4. Insert child list 4-5 between 2 and 3 (as per L2). After insertion, list: 1→2→4→5→3. Set current = 2.next = 4. Step 3: current = 4, no child, move to 5. Step 4: current = 5, no child, move to 3. Step 5: current = 3, no child, move to null. Traversal complete.

Start at a. Step 1: current = a, has child b. Insert child list b-c after a (since a.next is null). After insertion, list: a→b→c. Set current = a.next = b. Step 2: current = b, no child, move to c. Step 3: current = c, no child, move to null.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Update Pointers in Doubly Linked List Node
ladders.desc: Fundamental operation of modifying next and prev pointers of a node.
ladders.explanation: This is a basic building block for linked list manipulation. In a doubly linked list, each node has two pointers: next (to the following node) and prev (to the previous node). Updating these pointers correctly is essential to maintain list integrity. The intuition is that changing one pointer often requires updating another to keep the list consistent. For example, if we set node A.next = B, we must also set B.prev = A (if B is not null). The algorithm for a simple update: given nodes A and B, to insert B after A: store old_next = A.next; set A.next = B; set B.prev = A; set B.next = old_next; if old_next is not null: set old_next.prev = B. Time and space are O(1). Connection to L3: This operation is used repeatedly in the traversal and insertion logic. Common mistakes include forgetting to update prev pointers (leading to broken links), not handling null cases, or updating pointers in wrong order (causing data loss). Real code pattern: def insert_after(A, B): if not A: return; temp = A.next; A.next = B; if B: B.prev = A; B.next = temp; if temp: temp.prev = B.

examples.input: List: X - Y, insert Z after X
examples.trace: Start: X→Y, Y←X. Step 1: Store old_next = Y. Step 2: Set X.next = Z. Step 3: Set Z.prev = X. Step 4: Set Z.next = Y. Step 5: Set Y.prev = Z. Result: X→Z→Y, with prev: X←Z←Y.

examples.input: List: A - null, insert B after A
examples.trace: Start: A→null. Step 1: Store old_next = null. Step 2: Set A.next = B. Step 3: Set B.prev = A. Step 4: Set B.next = null. Step 5: No update to old_next.prev. Result: A→B, with prev: A←B.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Doubly Linked List Node Structure
ladders.desc: Understanding the basic structure of a node in a doubly linked list.
ladders.explanation: This is the simplest prerequisite concept. A doubly linked list is a linear data structure where each node contains three components: data (value), a next pointer (reference to the next node), and a prev pointer (reference to the previous node). The intuition is that this allows traversal in both directions, unlike singly linked lists. The node structure is fundamental because all operations on the list involve manipulating these pointers. For the multilevel list problem, nodes also have a child pointer (which can point to another doubly linked list head), but at this foundation level, we focus on the basic next and prev. A node can be visualized as: [prev | data | next]. The list starts with a head (prev = null) and ends with a tail (next = null). Time and space for accessing a node are O(1). Connection to L4: This structure is what we update when modifying pointers. Common mistakes include not initializing pointers to null, or confusing next and prev. Real code pattern: class Node: def __init__(self, val): self.val = val; self.prev = null; self.next = null; self.child = null.

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize a Node object. Step 2: Set val = 5. Step 3: Set prev = null. Step 4: Set next = null. Step 5: Optionally set child = null. Result: a single node with no connections.

examples.input: Link two nodes: A(val=1) and B(val=2)
examples.trace: Step 1: Create node A with val=1, prev=null, next=null. Step 2: Create node B with val=2, prev=null, next=null. Step 3: Set A.next = B. Step 4: Set B.prev = A. Result: A→B and A←B.

testCases.input: Link nodes a, b, c in sequence
